retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Tertiary syphilis is a destructive stage that manifests months or years after the initial infection in patients who have not received effective treatment during the primary or secondary stages of the disease. Oral manifestations of this phase include a chronic granulomatous gumma usually located on the hard palate, which may perforate into the nasal septum. The tongue may present with leukoplakia dorsally or appear atrophic and fissured. [5] [19] Tertiary syphilis symptoms result from complications of previous stages. They include Argyll Robertson pupil that constricts with accommodation but is not reactive to light, aortitis due to the vasa vasorum destruction, and neurosyphilis, such as tabes dorsalis. Congenital syphilis is transferred from an infected mother to the fetus, usually after 16 weeks of pregnancy. Before this period, Langerhans cells prevent the transmission of the spirochete to the fetus. As the fetus develops, the number of Langerhans cells decreases, allowing the passage of the spirochetes. When pregnant women transmit syphilis to their children, various developmental defects commonly arise, including dental abnormalities. Hutchinson incisors are characteristic of congenital syphilis patients, small, widely spaced, and peg-shaped incisors; semitranslucent rather than ivory, with a screwdriver-shaped incisal edge. Another dental abnormality that may be seen is Moon molars or mulberry molars, where molar anatomy is replaced by small, dome-shaped teeth, with dental cusps set closer together. Mulberry molars have also been associated with enamel hypoplasia. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Scarlet Fever -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough skin examination is essential, as a fine, blanching, maculopapular erythematous rash accompanied by a strawberry tongue strongly suggests scarlet fever. [46] The rash associated with scarlet fever typically appears 2 to 3 days after the onset of infection but can be delayed for up to 7 days. The rashes generally begin on the trunk, underarms, and groin, spreading to the extremities while sparing the palms and soles. [46] The area around the mouth remains pale, creating a distinctive "circumoral pallor." A characteristic strawberry tongue develops, initially presenting with a white membrane on the tongue and enlarged, protruding papillae, referred to as a "white strawberry tongue." As the membrane sloughs off, the papillae persist, giving the tongue a red, strawberry-like appearance. [46] Pastia lines are linear clusters of papules observed in skin folds or pressure points, such as the neck, antecubital fossa, and groin. After the initial rash subsides, a phase of skin desquamation may occur, sometimes lasting up to 2 weeks. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Arcanobacterium haemolyticum Infections -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: An erythematous rash is present in many cases, mostly likely due to a toxin similar to that which causes scarlet fever in S pyogenes pharyngitis. [20] This rash manifests as diffuse erythematous blanching papules, often described as "sandpaper-like" and may be pruritic. The rashes typically start on the distal extremities and spread centrally, primarily affecting the neck, chest, and back while sparing the face, palms, and soles. Pharyngitis usually precedes the rash by 1 to 4 days and lasts for 2 to 5 days. [20] Clinically, symptoms can be indistinguishable from the rash of scarlet fever due to S pyogenes , although features such as strawberry tongue or Pastia lines are absent. [9] [7] Furthermore, some cases of A haemolyticum infection can have complications such as Lemierre syndrome. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Scarlet fever is typically associated with acute pharyngitis with accompanying symptoms such as sore throat, fever, odynophagia, and cervical adenopathy. The skin presents with a papular-blanching rash covering most of the body. This rash is described as a "sandpaper rash" because of the lesions' lack of confluence. It develops on the face, trunk, underarms, and groin first, spreading to the extremities later, sparing the palms and soles. Scarlet fever has a common oral manifestation known as "strawberry tongue" because of the hyperplastic fungiform papillae and white coating; as the white coating resolves, the remaining papules give the tongue a red, bumpy appearance. The throat may also appear erythematous along with white or yellowish patches, making swallowing painful. [7] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Angular Chelitis -- History and Physical -- Syndromic Presentations
retrieved_docs.content: Ariboflavinosis (vitamin B2 deficiency): Cheilosis, angular cheilitis, photosensitivity, magenta glossitis, stomatitis, pharyngitis, and pseudo-syphilis (seborrhea-like dermatitis of the scrotum, vulva, philtrum, or nasolabial folds). As iron absorption may be impaired, this deficiency can also lead to normocytic anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Argyll Robertson Pupil -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The condition usually occurs with other features of tertiary syphilis, which is how it is diagnosed and treated. As the underlying etiology is an STI, syphilis is spread from contact with the bodily fluids of a person with syphilis, typically during sexual contact with a sore (chancre) or from a mother to a fetus. Less common routes of transmission exist as well.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- History and Physical -- Other Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia due to underlying B12 deficiency can present with pallor, fatigue, and signs of congestive heart failure in severe cases. Glossitis may be observed as a beefy red tongue and occurs due to the loss of tongue papillae. A yellow-lemon tint due to jaundice may also be observed.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossitis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for glossitis is extremely broad, and can be slightly narrowed down according to the physical exam findings: Normal-appearing tongue: Burning mouth syndrome Diabetic neuropathy Post-herpetic glossitis Acid reflux Atrophic glossitis: Protein-calorie malnutrition Vitamin B12 deficiency Bullous disease Candidiasis Xerostomia [16] Median rhomboid glossitis: Haemangioma Geographic tongue Amyloidosis Candidiasis Squamous cell carcinoma [17] Strawberry tongue: Yellow fever Kawasaki disease Toxic shock syndrome [15] Geographic Tongue: Oral lichen planus Chemical or inhalational irritant (smoking) Dehydration Candidiasis Connective tissue disease Bullous disease Leukoplakia [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Pharyngitis -- History and Physical -- Group A β-hemolytic Streptococcus
retrieved_docs.content: Classic physical examination findings are exudative tonsillopharyngitis, enlarged tender anterior cervical lymph nodes, petechiae on the palate, and a strawberry tongue. Additionally, some patients may have a fine papular rash that begins in the groin and axilla, followed by spread to the trunk and extremities. The affected areas eventually undergo desquamation. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital and Maternal Syphilis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Facial changes might include frontal bossing (Olympian brown), saddle nose, and short maxilla.

retrieved_docs.title: Tropical Sprue -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Exam findings: Glossitis, cheilitis, stomatitis, pedal edema, abdominal distention

retrieved_docs.title: Glossitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are numerous potential etiologies for glossitis. These include: 1-Anemia: Iron-deficiency anemia Pernicious anemia [4] 2-Vitamin B deficiencies: Vitamin B1 Vitamin B2 Vitamin B3 Vitamin B6 Vitamin B9 Vitamin B12 [5] 3-Infections: Viral: herpes viruses, as well as post-herpetic glossitis Bacterial: rare in immunocompetent patients [6] Fungal: most commonly Candida species [7] Parasite: malaria, spirochetes [8] [9] 4-Medications: ACE inhibitors Albuterol Organosulfur antimicrobial drugs such as (sulphanilamide, sulphathiazole) [10] Oral contraceptive pills [11] Lithium carbonate [11] 5-Others: Psychological factors (conversion disorders, anxiety) Exposure to irritants, for example, alcohol, spicy food, and tobacco. Normal familial variants (fissured tongue, geographic tongue) Mechanical irritation (burns, chronic dental trauma) Poor hydration Down syndrome Psoriasis and other autoimmune conditions Burning mouth syndrome

retrieved_docs.title: Yersinia Pseudotuberculosis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Other problems associated with Yersinia pseudotuberculosis infection include terminal ileitis and intussusception, more commonly in children. In most cases, enterocolitis lasts one to three weeks. Erythema nodosum, arthralgias, reactive arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and lumbar facet joint disease can occur. A sporotrichoid pattern of disease has been associated with Yersinia pseudotuberculosis infection. Cutaneous manifestations include erythema of the face and neck, conjunctival hyperemia, strawberry tongue, punctate skin rash, and desquamation. Ophthalmic findings such as uveitis and conjunctivitis have also been reported. Physical findings caused by Yersinia pseudotuberculosis infection may be grouped into three main categories: systemic, enteric, and rheumatologic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tonsillitis -- Complications -- Systemic Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Scarlet fever This toxin-mediated illness from GABHS is characterized by a fine sandpaper-like rash and a “strawberry” tongue and may coexist with streptococcal tonsillitis. [45]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2688
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic factors, indicated by the allowed qualifier, are recognized as relevant contributors to several distinct medical conditions. Specifically, a genetic component is associated with التسمم الوشيقي (viral intoxication), اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (hyperthyroidism), and قرحة قلاعية (aphthous ulcer). The presence of a genetic predisposition suggests that hereditary influences may play a role in the development or susceptibility to each of these disorders, underscoring the importance of considering family history and genetic screening in their assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460
retrieved_docs.content: التحصّي is associated with other male genital disorders, indicating a connection between this condition and various other disorders affecting the male genitalia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2771
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier is relevant in the epidemiological context of several disorders, including قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), and تمزق الشبكية (retinal detachment). Recognizing that the allowed qualifier pertains to the epidemiology of these conditions highlights the importance of monitoring their occurrence, distribution, and risk factors within populations, thereby supporting public‑health planning, disease surveillance, and targeted preventive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain, a symptom that lacks a clearly defined cause, has been reported to be clinically associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). This relationship suggests that patients who present with unexplained chest discomfort may warrant evaluation for an underlying fungal infection of the oral cavity, particularly when other risk factors for candidiasis are present. Recognizing this association can help clinicians adopt a more comprehensive diagnostic approach and ensure appropriate treatment for both the chest pain and any concurrent oral candidiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both anxiety (القلق) and oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). Anxiety can manifest as vague chest discomfort, making it an important psychosomatic contributor to this symptom. Oral candidiasis, a fungal infection of the mouth, may also be linked to nonspecific chest pain, potentially through systemic effects, medication side‑effects, or underlying immunosuppression that predisposes patients to both conditions. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may signal either anxiety or oral thrush—or a combination of both—helps clinicians pursue a more comprehensive evaluation and tailor management to address the underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1726
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق) is characterized by fatigue (التَّعَب) and may present with nonspecific chest pain. Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is also clinically associated with both nonspecific chest pain and fatigue. The overlap of fatigue and nonspecific chest pain in anxiety and oral candidiasis highlights the need for careful clinical assessment to differentiate between psychological and infectious etiologies, ensuring appropriate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a category that includes several distinct conditions. It is classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery). Within this category are the specific complications zwangerschap; vermoeidheid (pregnancy‑related fatigue) and طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال (multiform rash of pregnancy). These entities represent various adverse events that can occur during gestation, underscoring the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and management of substance use, fatigue, and dermatologic manifestations to optimize maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified conditions related to pregnancy comprise a range of distinct gestational disorders. This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1923
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is a medical condition that is associated with ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is linked to several findings, including النفخة الانقباضية, حفظ الصحة الروتيني, عرج, and لغط الشريان السباتي. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype called التصلب العصيدي المعمم, reflecting a more generalized form of the disease. These associations are important for recognizing the clinical presentation of التصلب العصيدي and guiding appropriate evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: adverse‑event reporting and clinical management. Recognizing sodium monofluorophosphate as a fluoride source guides oral‑health preventive strategies, while awareness of the SMQ classification aids clinicians in systematic identification and treatment of functional GI disturbances, underscoring the importance of these entities for patient care and product design.
